author: @aquahoodjd
text:
So I want to stack a bunch of those flywheels up in a vertical stack with a central perhaps variable drive shaft so you can dump energy into one two three four or five of them. And when you have both wind and solar energy that successive that you don't need as much as you need for any given time. That it starts to load a very powerful spring. It's slowly using gears or as Archimedes has said you know a lever.... And perhaps use magnets to help reduce the drag of the flywheels at least at the bottom by arranging magnets in a configuration that holds the flywheels and the center drive shaft inside a concentric field and have it either balanced on a very durable point made of tungsten inside of a tungsten cone shaped thought with some type of lubricant or a bearing of some sort so that there's a little resistance and drag on the flywheels as possible so that we can dump as much of the springs power into the flywheels and at different speeds depending on what's necessary and how much has been stored. And then of course the flywheels run a generator or themselves have stators built within them or in between them. You could make very small and small back comparison battery storage units that are mechanical and virtually quiet that could be in the garage or basement a backyard or virtually anywhere and would require no chemical battery and it's kind of like the gravity method but much smaller much more compact and frankly modular so that it could be scaled up or kept small for one person's use or one places use or you could attach multiple units that are basically the size of a person self-contained and you plug it in as you would a battery into your alternative power source generator whether it be an air turbine solar power or anything else It dumps all of the excess load into a mechanical spring and my question is what would be the best way to reduce drag and what types of metal or what types of spring do you imagine would be the most efficient as well as how to control the output of the spring so that it's used more consistently and only releases the amount of stored energy that's necessary. 

Batteries are incredibly expensive especially batteries with high density not to mention that lithium polymer batteries can be very dangerous fire hazards as I woke up just in time one morning and one was smoking and thank goodness I quickly wrapped it and kept on tape and threw it in a cold water oxygen sealed environment in the form of a Swiss Army ammo box filled with cold water and no more oxygen for it to use and put it outside on a very cold surface so bleeding off the thermal runaway.

author: @delburtphend6016
text: You can warp a flywheel with a weighted string, and then if you spin the flywheel and release the mass on the end of the string, you will get a massive increase in energy. If the wheel has 20 times the mass of the thrown mass then the thrown mass will have 20 times the energy after it is thrown.1/2 * 20 kg * 1 m/sec * 1m/sec = 10 J. 1/2 * 1 kg * 20 m/sec * 20 m/sec = 200 J

author: @jcl5m
text: Hi, i've been curious about fly-ring concepts vs fly-wheel because the kinetic energy proportional to square of the square of radius and mass distirbution. YT keeps recommending your videos when I do my background research.  I would love to get your thoughts on the idea if you are open.   Much of the mass of the unform flywheel is near the middle which doesn't contribute as much to the stored energy.  Flywheels which have thinned out centers are limited by the tensile strength of the spokes are higher velocities.  A fly-ring in tension , perhaps even supported externally would optimize mass distribution, radius, and potentially support higher velocities.  One challenge is a low-friction hubless support system for the ring.  If a modular ring support were possible, it would allow scaling to very large rings.  Would working on a project like this be interesting to you.  I might be willing to fund the prototype/exploration since I haven't found much information online.

author: @dongraham4760
text: Oh dear !! You keep going on about the voltage your barmy projects produce . Voltage alone has never done a days work in history .Current times voltage equals watts , watts is the real measure of work done !  This ill designed "flywheel " producing 10 volts at 5 milliamps or .05 watts and even that is doubtfull because when you measured the current with your meter , that puts a load on the system and I am sure the voltage would have sagged significantly ,approaching zero at the time of measuring so the true output would also be approaching zero . Why ?   Because the coil around the circumstance of the flywheel is useless . The magnetic fields from the magnetics need to cut the coil or coils perpendicular to their placement , your "coil" will NEVER work to even extract the smallest fraction of the work put in . The "voltage " and "Current" you are seeing is being generated from the field lines cutting along the length of individual wires of the coil and leaking out which you measure  , a more inefficient way of producing power I do not know of!

author: @kevinbuiied
text:
This has some call backs to a Transverse Flus Generator. For N numbers of slots in the bike, you should have 2*N magnets, alternating North and South. All the Norths should land on a slot at the same time, and as it rotates, all the south should also land on the slots. So in an alternating fashion, all the norths cross, and then all the souths cross.

If possible, I would say to have the slot width and the tab widths be equivalent, so that a N and S magnet pair do not cross over a tab or slot at the same time. You want all the north flux crossing over a tab at the same time, while the south flux passes through the slot, and vice versa.

author: @IstasPumaNevada
text: Source of energy loss is not only bearings of course, but also air resistance/air friction. And the losses due to air resistance/friction increase exponentially with faster rotation speed while the bearing losses increase roughly linearly. With good bearings at high speeds I would imagine the losses to air resistance are much higher than from the bearings, so it's always a good idea to make sure the flywheel itself is as smooth and uninterrupted in shape as possible.

author: @_SimpleSam
text:
You want the flux perpendicular to coil.
So, I would think leaving the floor of the rim uncut, and cutting the edge of the rim to bend out and around then in to the center might be better.
The idea being that the north south flux travels into the tab, around the floor of the rim, and then back down the other side tab to the next door opposing magnet.
Since the tabs from the rim wouldn't reach all the way across, it would probably make sense to use the entire rim, and stagger the tabs, either side.

I would think that arrangement would make a world of difference.
That make sense?

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
Presumably you could also tune the output, volts & amps, somewhat by having 2 norths & 2 souths ( or 4 of each etc )

The thing that I think would be really interesting to explore is decoupling the coil from the cage. The reason being, if you could do that then you could contra rotate the magnets and cage but have a stationary wire electric output  no slip-rings/brushes to wear and maintain in a contra-rotating dual wind turbine.

My aim there is to get something useful from the lowest wind speeds,  with least compromise from medium to higher windspeeds eg have the cage rotating on the lowest start up windspeed turbine and the magnets on a more medium speed one. 

Maybe even experiment with different types of VAWT eg Drag inner and lift outer ( Ugrinsky centre and lift type outer? ) I know it seems a bit like having your cake and eating it but I'm being optimistic and seeing it more as,  eating your cake slowly while the next one's being made ;-)

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text: Combining the contra rotating magnets/cage with a tapped coil ( that switches in the full coil above a certain level of volts out ) would further lower the start up windspeed needed to get something out, but not negatively hamper the medium/top speed outputs.

I had an idea a while back, but never found the time to verify it. 

2×2 meters plywood .25" made in an X  .75×3.5". Put 5 kilo's at each end of the plywood. If you're  creative, use something aerodynamic for the weights. author: @stevestogsdill5791
text: With the flywheel bus as diagramed above, does the flywheel have and additional benefit of gyro stability to the bus? Also, isn't this the tech behind F1's KERS units?

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text:
Two variants I know of were used on Swiss buses, the one on the diagram @4:40 which powers an electric motor did spin really fast, and had some strong gyroscopic effect which was a problem because inclines would put stress on the bearings, which incidentally had a bad habit of failing prematurely. What would be interesting to know is if the gyroscopic forces would be sufficient to keep the bus upright when the right wheels are in mid-air over the edge of a mountain road 😀
The other more recent variant with flywheel was used on gas/diesel buses as a purely mechanical way to recover energy from breaking, and getting it back when accelerating. I recall the bus company stating that it saved around 30% in fuel, which is significant. That was in the 80's, I don't know if they still use them now.

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text: I've been fascinated by flywheels, ever since helping to start an old Stuart Turner engine. You have to spin up a massive flywheel before releasing a clutch to turn the engine over. It takes longer than hand-cranking a (very) old car engine, but it gets easier and easier as it gathers momentum and doesn't need a special grip to avoid a broken thumb!
